title: Hash Maps & Hashing
description: A powerful data structure that uses mathematical functions to map keys directly to their values for extremely fast operations.

sections.title: Introduction to Hash Maps
sections.content: A Hash Map (or Hash Table) is a data structure that implements an associative array abstract data type, a structure that can map keys to values. Hash maps are incredibly popular because, on average, they provide constant time $O(1)$ complexity for lookups, insertions, and deletions.

sections.title: The Hash Function & Modulo Operator
sections.content:
To store a key-value pair, the Hash Map needs to figure out exactly which 'bucket' (array index) to put it in. It does this in two steps:

### 1. The Hash Function
The string key is passed through a mathematical function that converts it into a large integer (the hash code). For example, summing the ASCII values of the characters.

### 2. The Modulo Operation
Because arrays have a fixed size, the large integer is reduced to fit within the array bounds using the modulo operator (`%`). 
Formula: `target_bucket = hash_code % array_length`.

sections.title: Collisions & Separate Chaining
sections.content:
What happens when two completely different keys calculate to the exact same bucket? This is called a **Collision**.

### Separate Chaining
One of the most common ways to resolve collisions is 'Separate Chaining'. Instead of storing the value directly in the bucket, each bucket holds a reference to another data structure (usually an array or linked list). When a collision occurs, the new key-value pair is simply appended to the chain in that specific bucket.

### The Worst Case Scenario
If the hash function is poor and maps *every* key to the exact same bucket, the Hash Map degrades into a single long chain. In this worst-case scenario, searching through the chain takes $O(n)$ time.

quiz.question: What is the average time complexity for searching, inserting, and deleting in a Hash Map?
quiz.explanation: Hash Maps use hash functions to jump directly to the target bucket, allowing for constant $O(1)$ time complexity on average.

quiz.question: What is a 'collision' in the context of a Hash Map?
options: When the Hash Map runs out of memory.
When two different keys compute to the exact same bucket index.
When a key is deleted while it is being searched.
When the modulo operator returns a negative number.
quiz.explanation: A collision occurs when the hash function and modulo operation assign two distinct keys to the same index in the underlying array.

quiz.question: How does 'Separate Chaining' resolve collisions?
options: It deletes the old key and replaces it with the new key.
It searches for the next available empty bucket in the array.
It stores multiple key-value pairs in the same bucket using a secondary data structure like a list.
It forces the array to double in size immediately.
quiz.explanation: Separate chaining allows buckets to hold a list of items, so colliding pairs simply get appended to that bucket's list.
